0:
{
  "report": "Rule ID: gitlab.find_sec_bugs.PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN-1
Message: A file is opened to read its content. The filename comes from an input parameter. If an
unfiltered parameter is passed to this file API, files from an arbitrary filesystem location
could be read. This rule identifies potential path traversal vulnerabilities. In many cases,
the constructed file path cannot be controlled by the user.

Location: /data/jiangjiajun/LLM4Security/data/projects/spark/src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:67:gitlab.find_sec_bugs.PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN-1",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "The report is a true positive. The sink is real: `ExternalResource.getInputStream()` opens whatever `File` object was built from the constructor path (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:39-40,66-67`). That constructor is reachable from an HTTP-controlled path in the external static-file feature: `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` reads the request path (`src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:40-59`), `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)` appends it to the configured base directory and creates `new ExternalResource(addedPath)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:60-70`), and `StaticFilesConfiguration.consumeWithFileResourceHandlers()` later serves it with `resource.getInputStream()` (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:91-103`). So this is not an unreachable or purely theoretical flow (not A1/A2, not B1). The code does have a traversal defense, but it is incomplete rather than eliminating the issue. `UriPath.canonical()` only canonicalizes `/`-separated segments (`src/main/java/spark/resource/UriPath.java:30-144`), while `StringUtils.cleanPath()` later converts backslashes to `/` and resolves `..` (`src/main/java/spark/utils/StringUtils.java:218-255`). Then `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal()` checks only whether the normalized absolute path string starts with the normalized base-folder string (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:18-33`). That prefix check has no path-boundary validation, so a path outside the base folder but in a sibling directory with the same prefix still passes. Because of that, attacker-controlled input can escape the intended directory and reach the file-open sink.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Relevant flow: request URL path -> `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(request)` -> `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String path)` -> `new ExternalResource(addedPath)` -> `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource)` -> `StaticFilesConfiguration.consumeWithFileResourceHandlers()` -> `resource.getInputStream()`.\n\nKey code:\n1. HTTP input source: `String pathInContext = addPaths(servletPath, pathInfo); return getResource(pathInContext);` in `src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:58-59`.\n2. File path construction: `path = UriPath.canonical(path); final String addedPath = addPaths(baseResource, path); ExternalResource resource = new ExternalResource(addedPath);` in `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:66-70`.\n3. Normalization inside constructor: `file = new File(StringUtils.cleanPath(path));` in `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:39-40`.\n4. Incomplete guard: `return rlatsPath.startsWith(rlatsFolder);` in `src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:30-33`.\n5. Sink: `return new FileInputStream(file);` in `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:66-67`.\n\nConcrete exploit scenario: if an application configures `staticFiles.externalLocation(\"/var/www/static\")` and a sibling directory `/var/www/static_backup/` exists, a request path like `/%2e%2e%5cstatic_backup/secret.txt` can reach the sink. `UriPath.canonical()` does not collapse the `..\\` segment, but `cleanPath()` converts it to `/../static_backup/secret.txt` and normalizes the final file path to `/var/www/static_backup/secret.txt`. The traversal check then wrongly accepts it because `/var/www/static_backup/secret.txt`.startsWith(`/var/www/static`) is true, even though the file is outside `/var/www/static`. The framework then streams the file via `getInputStream()`.\n\nThe existing external traversal test does not disprove this. `src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTestExternal.java:120-125` only checks a non-existing path and expects 404; in current code the traversal check runs only when `resource.exists()` is true (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:81-83`).",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

{
  "report": "Rule ID: gitlab.find_sec_bugs.PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN-1
Message: A file is opened to read its content. The filename comes from an input parameter. If an
unfiltered parameter is passed to this file API, files from an arbitrary filesystem location
could be read. This rule identifies potential path traversal vulnerabilities. In many cases,
the constructed file path cannot be controlled by the user.

Location: /data/jiangjiajun/LLM4Security/data/projects/spark/src/main/java/spark/utils/ResourceUtils.java:182:gitlab.find_sec_bugs.PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN-1",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "This finding is a false positive. The reported line in `ResourceUtils.getFile(String)` does not open or read a file; it only constructs and returns a `File` object from the provided string. That means the Semgrep rule selected the wrong sink for the claimed behavior, which matches Type B1. In addition, project-level reachability does not support exploitability: searching the codebase shows that this `getFile(String)` overload has no production callers in `src/main/java`; only URL-based overloads are used internally, and test-only calls exist in `src/test/java`. The real request-driven static-file flow goes through `AbstractResourceHandler` -> `ExternalResourceHandler` / `ClassPathResourceHandler`, where the request path is canonicalized and checked for traversal before any file stream is opened. So the reported location is not a real CWE-022 issue in this project.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1.Incorrect Source/Sink Selection"
  ],
  "evidence": "Flagged code: `src/main/java/spark/utils/ResourceUtils.java:163-183` ends with `return new File(resourceLocation);` at line 182. This is object construction only; there is no `FileInputStream`, `Files.read*`, or similar file-read sink in this method. Code search shows no production use of `ResourceUtils.getFile(String)`; matches are only `AbstractFileResolvingResource.java:46,58` for the URL overloads and tests in `src/test/java/spark/util/ResourceUtilsTest.java`. The actual HTTP static-resource path is `StaticFilesConfiguration.consumeWithFileResourceHandlers()` (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:85-103`) -> `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:40-58`) -> `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:60-83`) or `ClassPathResourceHandler.getResource(String)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResourceHandler.java:61-84`). In those handlers, the user-controlled path is first normalized with `UriPath.canonical(path)` and then checked with `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(...)` / `protectAgainstInClassPath(...)` before the file is opened. The actual file-open operation is in `ExternalResource.getInputStream()` as `new FileInputStream(file)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:66-67`), and that path is guarded by the traversal checks.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

{
  "report": "Rule ID: gitlab.find_sec_bugs.PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN-1
Message: A file is opened to read its content. The filename comes from an input parameter. If an
unfiltered parameter is passed to this file API, files from an arbitrary filesystem location
could be read. This rule identifies potential path traversal vulnerabilities. In many cases,
the constructed file path cannot be controlled by the user.

Location: /data/jiangjiajun/LLM4Security/data/projects/spark/src/main/java/spark/utils/ResourceUtils.java:143:gitlab.find_sec_bugs.PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN-1",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report is a false positive. At the flagged location in `spark/utils/ResourceUtils.java:143`, the code does not open or read a file; it only converts a string into a `file:` URL with `new File(resourceLocation).toURI().toURL()`. That is not, by itself, a CWE-022 sink. In Spark’s actual request-driven static-file serving path, user-controlled request paths go through `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` and then `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)` or `ClassPathResourceHandler.getResource(String)`, where the path is canonicalized and checked by directory-traversal guards before any `InputStream` is opened. So the finding is not a real exploitable path traversal in this project.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "Flagged code (`spark/utils/ResourceUtils.java:137-144`): `try { return new URL(resourceLocation); } catch (MalformedURLException ex) { return new File(resourceLocation).toURI().toURL(); }`. This only creates a `URL` object and does not access file contents. Repo-wide usage shows no main-code callers of `ResourceUtils.getURL(String)` or `ResourceUtils.getFile(String)` from HTTP request handling. The real file read for static resources is `spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:66-68`: `return new FileInputStream(file);`. That read is reached through `spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:40-59` and `spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:66-83` / `spark/resource/ClassPathResourceHandler.java:67-84`, where `path = UriPath.canonical(path);` is applied and then `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource);` or `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstInClassPath(resource.getPath(), baseResource);` runs before the resource is returned. Therefore the reported line is not the real sink, and the real request path has traversal checks.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}
2:
{
  "report": "Rule ID: gitlab.find_sec_bugs.PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN-1
Message: A file is opened to read its content. The filename comes from an input parameter. If an
unfiltered parameter is passed to this file API, files from an arbitrary filesystem location
could be read. This rule identifies potential path traversal vulnerabilities. In many cases,
the constructed file path cannot be controlled by the user.

Location: /data/jiangjiajun/LLM4Security/data/projects/spark/src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesFolder.java:28:gitlab.find_sec_bugs.PATH_TRAVERSAL_IN-1",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The finding at `StaticFilesFolder.java:28` is a false positive. The reported method, `StaticFilesFolder.externalConfiguredTo(String folder)`, is a deprecated configuration helper, not a request handler. At the flagged line it only computes `Paths.get(folder).toAbsolutePath().toString().replace(\"\\\\\", \"/\")` and stores that string in a static field; it does not open or read any file. So the report's sink selection is incorrect for CWE-022. The `folder` argument is also a developer-supplied startup configuration value, not attacker-controlled request input in the normal Spark usage model: external static directories are configured through `Service.externalStaticFileLocation(...)` / `Spark.externalStaticFileLocation(...)` before the app starts serving requests. The actual request-driven file access path is elsewhere: `MatcherFilter.doFilter` -> `StaticFilesConfiguration.consume` -> `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` -> `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)` -> `ExternalResource.getInputStream()`. On that real path, request paths are canonicalized with `UriPath.canonical(...)`, validated against the configured base directory with `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(...)`, and traversal attempts are turned into HTTP 400 responses. Therefore the reported location does not represent an exploitable path traversal vulnerability.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],
  "evidence": "At the reported location, `src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesFolder.java` lines 26-30 only normalize and store a path string: `String unixLikeFolder = Paths.get(folder).toAbsolutePath().toString().replace(\"\\\\\", \"/\"); ... external = removeLeadingAndTrailingSlashesFrom(unixLikeFolder);`. No file-reading API is called there. The actual file read is in `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java` lines 66-67: `return new FileInputStream(file);`. The request-driven flow reaches that sink through `src/main/java/spark/http/matching/MatcherFilter.java` line 98 (`staticFiles.consume(httpRequest, httpResponse)`), then `src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java` lines 91-103, and `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java` lines 65-83. In that handler, the request path is canonicalized at line 66 (`path = UriPath.canonical(path);`) and then checked at line 82 (`DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource);`). If traversal is detected, `StaticFilesConfiguration.consume` lines 74-79 returns HTTP 400. Also, the flagged `StaticFilesFolder` helper is deprecated and appears unused in this repository: code search finds no call sites for `StaticFilesFolder.localConfiguredTo`, `externalConfiguredTo`, `local()`, or `external()` outside their own definitions.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

The flagged code at `spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:27` is only a normalization helper: `Paths.get(path).toAbsolutePath().toString().replace(\"\\\\\", \"/\")`. It does not open or read a file, so the rule's claimed sink ('A file is opened to read its content') is incorrect. This is not mainly an A1/A2 reachability problem: the external static-file request path is reachable at runtime, and there is a real file-read sink elsewhere. Manual review shows a separate real CWE-022 risk in the external static-file flow because the containment check uses a raw string-prefix test, but that is not what Semgrep actually reported at line 27. Therefore the report itself is FP due to incorrect source/sink selection.",
